audrey-nell-hamilton-and-joeann-anderson.display_name: Audrey Nell Hamilton and JoeAnn Anderson Ulmer (joint interview)
audrey-nell-hamilton-and-joeann-anderson.role_summary: Joint oral history of two Mississippi civil rights movement participants; the surnames are not Whisper artifacts (the prior audit canon resolved 'Joanne' to 'JoeAnn' via the AUDIT_VS_LOC_DISAGREEMENTS report on entry 4).
audrey-nell-hamilton-and-joeann-anderson.ai_reading: The Hamilton/Ulmer joint interview's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 84), the Sunflower County Mississippi voter-registration director. Their next neighbors are Barbara Edna Vickers (entry 5, publication-block), Robert Bagner Hayling (entry 103, St. Augustine SCLC), Juadine Henderson (entry 68, Mississippi SNCC voter-registration), and Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan (entry 51, publication-block). The cluster reads them as Mississippi-rural-movement voices. Their +0.54 reading on the Sacred Framing vs Secular Framing axis fits the Black Mississippi church-anchored movement context, and the +0.59 reading on the Nonviolence vs Self-Defense axis joins the catalog's recurring inversion pattern of Southern-rural-Black-women's testimony landing toward the Self-Defense pole despite the nonviolent-protest framing the cultural reception applies.

simeon-booker-and-moses-newson.display_name: Simeon Booker and Moses Newson (joint interview)
simeon-booker-and-moses-newson.role_summary: Joint oral history of two pioneering Black journalists: Simeon Booker (1918-2017, Jet and Ebony Washington bureau chief from 1955), who covered the Emmett Till trial and the Freedom Rides; and Moses Newson (1927-2024), Baltimore Afro-American reporter who also covered both the Till trial and the 1961 Freedom Rides.
simeon-booker-and-moses-newson.ai_reading: The Booker/Newson interview's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 124), with C. T. Vivian (entry 134, SCLC), Annie Pearl Avery (entry 3, SNCC), Robert L. Carter (entry 108, NAACP LDF), and H. Jack Geiger (entry 54, Mississippi community health) rounding out the top five. The cluster is broadly civil-rights-network, with the embedding pulling their journalist-witness testimony as close to the SNCC field organizers they covered as to the NAACP legal infrastructure that supported the cases they reported. The Southern Struggle axis position of +0.53 reflects their Mississippi-Till-trial and Alabama-Freedom-Ride coverage anchor. The journalist's eye in their testimony reads similar to the Mississippi voter-registration cohort's testimony, the embedding noting that the chronicler and the chronicled share enough vocabulary for high cosine similarity.

candie-carawan-and-guy-hughes-carawan.display_name: Candie Carawan and Guy Hughes Carawan (joint interview)
candie-carawan-and-guy-hughes-carawan.role_summary: Joint oral history of Guy Carawan (1927-2015) and Candie Anderson Carawan, the Highlander Folk School music teachers who taught "We Shall Overcome" to SNCC and SCLC organizers in 1960 and shaped the corpus of movement freedom songs.
candie-carawan-and-guy-hughes-carawan.ai_reading: The Carawans' top semantic neighbor is Scott Bates (entry 116, count 118), the SCLC Educational Director cohort member; second is Jamila Jones (entry 58, count 81), one of the singers who learned the freedom-song corpus at Highlander; third is Dorothy Foreman Cotton (entry 29, SCLC Educational Director); fourth is Betty Garman Robinson (entry 7, SNCC Northern support); fifth is Pete Seeger (entry 97, count 42). The Highlander music-tradition cluster the catalog has now documented across multiple entries (Pete Seeger, Jamila Jones, Scott Bates, and the Carawans themselves) is at its tightest here, the Carawans as the central node of the triadic-plus-Bates-plus-Seeger freedom-song-pedagogy signature. Their axis positions are all moderate (range -0.18 to +0.41), placing them in the catalog's centrist-voice band.

john-and-jean-rosenberg.role_summary: Joint oral history of John and Jean Rosenberg, a civil rights legal/movement partnership; in this corpus's embedding their testimony sits at the MAXIMUM Southern Struggle reading (+1.00) in the entire 136-interview corpus, alongside top semantic neighbors Robert L. Carter (NAACP LDF) and Charles McLaurin (Mississippi SNCC).
john-and-jean-rosenberg.ai_reading: The Rosenbergs' position on the Southern Struggle vs Northern Struggle axis is +1.00, the absolute MAXIMUM toward Southern Struggle in the entire 136-interview corpus, the second-largest extremum the catalog has documented (after Elmer Dixon's -1.00 on the nonviolence axis and Michael McCarty's -1.00 on the Southern axis itself). Their top semantic neighbors are Robert L. Carter (entry 108, count 230), Charles McLaurin (entry 17, count 219), Richard Barry Sobol (entry 104, count 189, a civil rights attorney in Louisiana), Rosie Head (entry 111, Mississippi rural), and Simeon Booker and Moses Newson (entry 9, the journalist joint interview). The cluster reads as the deeply-Southern legal-civil-rights cohort, with the Sobol pairing particularly notable: Sobol was lead counsel on Sobol v. Perez (1968), the federal case that established Louisiana lawyers' right to represent civil rights defendants over local-bar objections. The embedding may be catching shared Louisiana-and-Mississippi legal-organizing vocabulary across the Rosenbergs and Sobol; their post-1965 work in Appalachia would be the additional regional dimension not captured by the catalog's five-axis projection.

joseph-howell-and-embry-howell.display_name: Joseph Howell and Embry Howell (joint interview)
joseph-howell-and-embry-howell.role_summary: Joint oral history of Joseph Howell and Embry Howell; the lowest single-entry neighbor counts in the audited joint-interview subset (peak 42), suggesting a short or topically focused transcript.
joseph-howell-and-embry-howell.ai_reading: The Howells' top semantic neighbors are Junius Williams (entry 72, count 42, Newark Black Power Conference organizer), Joan Trumpauer Mulholland (entry 60, Freedom Rider), Martha Prescod Norman Noonan (entry 83, SNCC field organizer), C. T. Vivian (entry 134, SCLC), and Kathleen Cleaver (entry 73, BPP Communications Secretary). The cluster crosses Northern Black Power, Southern direct-action, SNCC fieldwork, and the BPP, an unusually broad spread for a low-count interview. Their position on the Nonviolence as Theology vs Armed Self-Defense axis is +0.84, far toward Armed Self-Defense, joining the catalog's recurring pattern of joint and family-member testimonies landing on the Self-Defense side. Without external biographical sourcing in this iteration, the catalog notes the cross-cluster pattern as the structural observation: the Howells' transcript reads as a movement-bridge voice spanning factions that did not historically partner.
